K–2.title: Algorithms with Daily Routines
K–2.objective: Students will understand that an algorithm is a set of ordered steps by creating and explaining a daily routine sequence.
K–2.task: Students will sequence picture cards representing a daily routine (e.g., wake up, brush teeth, eat breakfast) and explain the order to a partner.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for correct sequencing language and reasoning.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create and explain their own 4-step routine.
K–2.udl: Provide visuals, verbal explanations, and physical movement options.
K–2.materials: Routine cards, chart paper, glue, crayons.
K–2.vocabulary: algorithm, steps, order, first, next, last
K–2.procedures:
1. Introduce the idea of steps.
2. Model a routine.
3. Students sequence cards.
4. Students explain to partner.
5. Share out.
K–2.differentiation: Use fewer steps for support. Add extra steps for challenge.

K–2.title: Loops with Clap Patterns
K–2.objective: Students will recognize repeated patterns as loops.
K–2.task: Students repeat clap-stomp patterns and identify how many times they repeat.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create their own pattern loop.
K–2.procedures:
1. Model pattern.
2. Repeat together.
3. Students create patterns.
4. Share.

K–2.title: Algorithms with Daily Routines
K–2.objective: Students will understand that an algorithm is a set of ordered steps by creating and explaining a daily routine sequence.
K–2.task: Students will sequence picture cards representing a daily routine and explain the order.

K–2.title: Loops with Clap Patterns
K–2.task: Students repeat and create clap patterns.

K–2.title: Fix the Puzzle (Debugging)
K–2.objective: Students will identify mistakes and fix them.
K–2.task: Students solve broken puzzles and explain fixes.

K–2.title: If-Then Decisions
K–2.objective: Students will understand simple if-then logic.
K–2.task: Students act out decisions (If raining → use umbrella).

K–2.title: Sequence Through Dance
K–2.objective: Students will follow and create sequences.
K–2.task: Students create dance steps in order.

3–5.title: Pattern Recognition with Emoji Sorting
3–5.objective: Students will identify patterns and sorting rules using emoji cards.
3–5.task: Students sort emoji cards into categories, explain their rule, and compare with a partner.
3–5.summativeAssessment: Students create a sorting chart and explain the rule.
3–5.udl: Visual supports, color coding, verbal explanation options.
3–5.procedures: Model sorting → Students sort → Compare → Explain.
3–5.differentiation: Fewer cards for support, multi-attribute sorting for challenge.
3–5.reflection: How did you decide your rule?

3–5.title: Algorithms with Snack Instructions
3–5.objective: Students will write clear step-by-step instructions.
3–5.task: Students write instructions to make a snack and test with a partner.
3–5.differentiation: Sentence starters or more complex recipes.

3–5.title: Debugging Code Cards
3–5.objective: Students will identify and fix simple errors.
3–5.task: Students examine incorrect step cards and fix them.
3–5.reflection: How did you find the error?

3–5.title: Loops in Scratch
3–5.objective: Students will use loops in Scratch.

3–5.title: If-Then Game Design
3–5.objective: Students will use conditionals in a simple game.
3–5.task: Students design a game using if-then rules.

9–12.title: Data Visualizations with Spreadsheets
9–12.objective: Students will analyze a small data set and create an appropriate chart in a spreadsheet application.
9–12.task: Students import a data table into a spreadsheet, calculate one summary insight, and build a chart that communicates a clear conclusion.
9–12.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students selected a chart type that matches the data and can explain why.
9–12.summativeAssessment: Students submit a completed spreadsheet with chart and a short written analysis.
9–12.udl: Provide a sample data set, step-by-step guide, and optional video directions.
9–12.materials: Computers, spreadsheet software, sample data set, guided notes.
9–12.vocabulary: data set, chart, visualization, analysis, trend
9–12.procedures:
1. Introduce data visualization principles.
2. Model importing data and creating a chart.
3. Students analyze a provided data set.
4. Students create a chart and written analysis.
5. Class discussion on chart choices.
9–12.differentiation: Provide a partially completed spreadsheet for support. Challenge advanced learners to compare two chart types.
9–12.reflection: Why was your chart the best choice for this data?
